acc_001: Just reviewed GPT-4o's new features. The multimodal reasoning is genuinely impressive — but the real unlock is sub-100ms latency for real-time apps. This changes the UX design space entirely.

Hot take: most 'AI startups' in 2024 are just wrappers around foundation models with 6 months of runway. The ones that survive will be the ones building proprietary training pipelines. The moat is data, not the prompt.

Spent the weekend benchmarking local LLMs on a 16GB M3 MacBook. Llama 3 70B (Q4) hits 18 tok/s. Perfectly usable for dev workflows. The era of running capable models on consumer hardware is officially here.

3 things that will define AI infra in 2025:
1. Speculative decoding becoming standard
2. KV cache compression hitting production
3. Multi-agent orchestration frameworks consolidating

Thread incoming 🧵
acc_002: ETH down 8% and my timeline is full of panic. Meanwhile I'm looking at on-chain data showing whale accumulation at these levels. Retail always sells the bottom to the people who know what they're looking at 👀
Unpopular opinion: 90% of DeFi 'yield' is just inflation from protocol token emissions. Real yield from actual fees? That's the only number that matters long term. Learn to read the difference.
Bitcoin dominance crossing 58%. We're in the part of the cycle where alts bleed against BTC. This isn't doom — it's exactly how accumulation phases look. Been through 3 of these. Patience.

The Uniswap v4 hooks architecture is genuinely underrated. Allows custom AMM logic without forking the protocol. Devs are building things on top of it that weren't possible 6 months ago. Worth following.
acc_003: We keep discussing AI safety as if the goal is to constrain a tool. But if we're building systems capable of genuine goal-directed behavior, 'constraining' is the wrong mental model entirely. The question isn't what it can't do — it's what it cares about.

An underappreciated asymmetry: the cost of being wrong about AI risk is civilization-ending. The cost of being overly cautious is slower economic growth. These are not symmetric error types. Why do we treat them as such?

Reading the latest interpretability paper from Anthropic. The finding that features are shared across model families suggests something about the structure of semantic space that's genuinely surprising. What does it mean for representations to 'converge'?

People ask if AI will take their job. More precise question: will AI make the *output* of your job cheaper than your salary? For most knowledge work, the answer is approaching yes faster than most institutions can adapt.
